revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[it:Gūji]][[ja:宮司]][[zh:宮司]]
'''Guji''' (宮司, also known as '''Miyazukasa''') is a Rank and title for [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] who oversee {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}}, including managing other priests and {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|qid=Q1047139|lt=Miko|1=Miko}}.
[[File:Kusakabe Kimbei 53. Shinto Priest.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Photograph of a Guji taken by {{ill|Kusakabe Kimbei|en|Kusakabe Kimbei|qid=Q1794526|lt=Kusakabe Kimbei|1=Kusakabe Kimbei}}, circa 1902.]]
== Overview == Historically, the term "Gu" (宮) referred to the residences of {{ill|Imperial family members|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial family members|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581068}}, and "Guji" (宮司) initially designated officials serving these residences, such as those at the imperial palaces of crown princes and empresses. Over time, the term shifted to refer to those responsible for shrine construction and taxation before becoming the designation for religious functionaries conducting shrine rituals.
Originally, the title of the head priest of a shrine was '''Kannushi''' (神主), but by the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the term "Guji" came into use. Some major shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|qid=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine|1=Atsuta Shrine}}, {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|qid=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}}, and others, appointed both a '''Daiguji''' (大宮司, Chief Priest) and a '''Shōguji''' (少宮司, Deputy Chief Priest).
Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, Guji referred to the heads of {{ill|Kankokuheisha|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankokuheisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} (government-supported shrines). Lower-Ranking shrines were led by "Shashi" (社司) for provincial shrines and "Shashō" (社掌) for village shrines. From 1871 to 1880, Ise Grand Shrine and the highest-Ranked shrines had both Daiguji and Shōguji, but later only Ise Grand Shrine retained these positions. Some shrines, such as Atsuta Shrine and {{ill|Meiji Shrine|en|Meiji Shrine|qid=Q287165|lt=Meiji Shrine|1=Meiji Shrine}}, also have an '''Assistant Guji''' (権宮司).
After World War II, with the abolition of the shrine Ranking system, Guji became the general term for a shrine's head priest. Assistant Guji can still be appointed for {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} under the regulations of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.
== Role of the Guji == Guji serve as the chief ritual officers and administrators of shrines, managing shrine affairs and overseeing priests and staff.
According to the regulations of the Association of Shinto Shrines, the Guji is the legal representative of the shrine as a {{ill|Religious Corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporation|1=religious corporation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7311343}}, and only the Guji may hold this position. Each shrine must appoint a Guji.
For shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines (except for the Daiguji of Ise Grand Shrine, who is appointed by imperial decree), the Guji is appointed by the association's head. Guji of Beppyo shrines must hold at least the Rank of "Meikai" (明階), while those of other shrines must have at least "Gonshōkai" (権正階) qualifications.
Many smaller shrines have only one Guji as their sole staff member, and many priests concurrently serve as Guji for multiple shrines.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://news.livedoor.com/article/detail/14446110/|title="The Profitability of Shrines Leading to Succession Disputes"|accessdate=2018-08-25|publisher=Livedoor}}</ref> The shrine where a Guji primarily resides is called their "Main Shrine" (本務神社), while additional shrines they oversee are "Concurrent Shrines" (兼務神社). This makes Guji the most numerous priestly Rank, surpassing lower-Ranked priests such as "Gon-negi" (権禰宜).
If a Guji cannot be appointed (e.g., due to a lack of successors), a "Temporary Acting Guji" (宮司代務者) is designated until a new Guji is installed. Often, this is an official from the local Shrine Office.
Retired priests with significant contributions to {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} may be granted the honorary title of "Honorary Guji" (名誉宮司) upon recommendation by their successor. Honorary Guji may participate in shrine rituals in their former official attire and advise the current Guji.
Independent (non-affiliated) shrines appoint their Guji according to their own regulations.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}}
